questions.q: What is the objective of k-means clustering?
options: To assign labels to test data based on labeled training examples
To partition n data points into k clusters by minimizing within-cluster variance
To identify the k most important features in the dataset
To rank clusters by their statistical significance
questions.explanation: k-means minimizes the sum of squared distances from each point to its cluster centroid: Σ_i Σ_(x ∈ C_i) ||x - μ_i||². It is an unsupervised method that iteratively assigns points to the nearest centroid and updates centroids. Convergence is guaranteed, but the result depends on initialization.

questions.q: What is a limitation of k-means clustering?
options: It cannot handle numerical data
It requires the user to specify k in advance and is sensitive to initialization and cluster shape assumptions
It always produces clusters of equal size
It is guaranteed to find the global optimum
questions.explanation: k-means assumes roughly spherical clusters of similar size and requires pre-specifying k. Different initializations can lead to different local optima. It is sensitive to outliers and cannot discover clusters of arbitrary shape. Techniques like k-means++ improve initialization; silhouette analysis helps choose k.

questions.q: What is hierarchical clustering and how does it differ from k-means?
options: Hierarchical clustering only works on labeled data; k-means is unsupervised
Hierarchical clustering builds a tree of clusters (dendrogram) by either merging or splitting points, providing a hierarchy without pre-specifying k
Hierarchical clustering is faster than k-means for all datasets
They are equivalent methods; the names just describe different implementations
questions.explanation: Agglomerative hierarchical clustering starts with individual points and merges the closest clusters (single-link, complete-link, average-link). Divisive approaches do the reverse. The dendrogram reveals hierarchical structure and can be cut at different heights to obtain different numbers of clusters. It is deterministic unlike k-means.

questions.q: What does DBSCAN stand for and what is its key advantage over k-means?
options: Data-Based Segmentation Clustering Algorithm; it always runs faster
Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise; it can discover arbitrary-shaped clusters and automatically identify outliers
Decision Boundary Scanning Clustering; it is supervised
Dynamic Batch Separation Clustering Algorithm; it works only on time-series data
questions.explanation: DBSCAN groups points that are density-connected (close together with sufficient neighbors). Points with fewer than minPts neighbors are marked as outliers. It discovers non-spherical clusters and does not require specifying the number of clusters. Parameters eps (radius) and minPts must be tuned.

questions.q: What is the elbow method used for in clustering?
options: To determine the optimal learning rate for gradient descent
To identify the optimal number of clusters k by plotting within-cluster variance against k and looking for an elbow-shaped bend
To remove outliers from the dataset before clustering
To validate that a cluster is spherical in shape
questions.explanation: The elbow method plots inertia (within-cluster sum of squares) vs. k. As k increases, inertia decreases, but the rate slows. The elbow is the k where diminishing returns begin, suggesting an optimal cluster count. This is heuristic and subjective; silhouette analysis provides a more quantitative alternative.

questions.q: What does the silhouette coefficient measure?
options: The number of points misclassified in a cluster
How well a point fits its assigned cluster compared to other clusters; values range from -1 to 1 with higher being better
The distance from a cluster centroid to the nearest neighboring cluster
The probability that the clustering is statistically significant
questions.explanation: Silhouette coefficient s(i) = (b(i) - a(i)) / max(a(i), b(i)) where a(i) is the average distance to points in the same cluster and b(i) is the average distance to the nearest neighboring cluster. Values near 1 indicate good clustering; near 0 indicate overlapping clusters; negative values indicate misclassification.

questions.q: What is the difference between single-linkage and complete-linkage hierarchical clustering?
options: Single-linkage is faster; complete-linkage is more accurate
Single-linkage uses the minimum distance between clusters; complete-linkage uses the maximum distance, reducing the chaining effect
Single-linkage requires labeled data; complete-linkage does not
Complete-linkage always produces exactly k clusters; single-linkage requires cutting the dendrogram
questions.explanation: Single-linkage d(C_i, C_j) = min{d(x, y) : x ∈ C_i, y ∈ C_j} tends to chain clusters together, merging distant points. Complete-linkage uses the maximum distance, favoring compact clusters. Average-linkage uses the mean distance. The choice affects the dendrogram shape and final clustering.

questions.q: How does eps (epsilon) affect DBSCAN's behavior?
options: It controls the number of clusters created; larger eps gives more clusters
It is the radius within which DBSCAN searches for neighbors; larger eps leads to fewer, larger clusters; smaller eps leads to more fragmentation and outliers
It is the minimum distance required between any two points
It is unrelated to the final clustering result
questions.explanation: eps is DBSCAN's neighborhood radius. Too large: all points merge into one cluster. Too small: most points become outliers. Tuning eps (often via k-distance graphs) is crucial. The choice depends on data density and the problem domain. minPts also affects whether points qualify as core or border points.

questions.q: What is the curse of dimensionality and how does it affect clustering?
options: It is a bug in clustering software that must be fixed
In high dimensions, distances become less meaningful because most points are equidistant from each other, degrading clustering quality
It refers to the maximum number of clusters that can be created
It ensures that clustering always produces interpretable results
questions.explanation: In high-dimensional spaces, the volume grows exponentially, making pairwise distances nearly uniform. This breaks distance-based metrics used by k-means, hierarchical clustering, and DBSCAN. Dimensionality reduction (PCA, t-SNE) or feature selection before clustering can help. The problem worsens as d increases without proportional data increase.

questions.q: When would you use agglomerative hierarchical clustering over k-means?
options: When you need to assign class labels to new points
When you need a dendrogram to understand hierarchical relationships, when k is unknown, or when clusters have non-spherical shapes
When the dataset is very large (millions of points)
When all features are categorical without numerical encoding
questions.explanation: Hierarchical clustering is preferable when interpretability of the cluster hierarchy matters or when k is uncertain (it can be determined post-hoc from the dendrogram). It handles arbitrary shapes well but is O(n² log n) or worse, making it slow for large datasets. k-means is faster for high-cardinality clustering.
